topic: I bought a used Subaru and the previous owner's saved GPS destinations all match addresses I will visit in the next eighteen months
title: I Bought a Used Subaru. Every Saved GPS Destination Is a Place I Will Visit in the Future. | True Scary Story
description_intro: I bought a used two thousand eighteen Subaru Outback at a dealership in Sacramento last March. The dashboard GPS held fourteen saved destinations from the previous owner. Eleven of them are addresses I would visit over the next eight months. I had never been to any of them before. The previous owner died last September. His name was Carl Bergquist. He kept a leather diary of intended trips.
description:
I bought a used two thousand eighteen Subaru Outback at a dealership in Sacramento last March. The dashboard GPS held fourteen saved destinations from the previous owner. Eleven of them are addresses I would visit over the next eight months. I had never been to any of them before. The previous owner died last September. His name was Carl Bergquist.

The dealership was a small independent lot on Folsom Boulevard. The salesman, a man named Mike Petros, told me the car had been a trade in from an estate executor. He did not know much else. The GPS had not been wiped. The fourteen saved destinations were labeled in plain text. A hospital in Roseville. A dentist on H Street. A florist in Davis. A wedding venue near Lake Tahoe. A cemetery outside Reno. Each one I would end up at within months of buying the car. None of them had ever been on my radar before.

I located Carl's sister in Stockton. She gave me a leather diary he had kept during his final year. The diary listed every destination on the GPS by address, with a planned arrival date next to each. The dates in the diary match the dates I actually arrived at each location. Watch to the end. There are three saved destinations I have not reached yet. The last one is a cemetery. The date next to it is eight months from now.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundtapes
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I bought a used Subaru Outback in Sacramento last March. The dashboard GPS held fourteen saved destinations from the previous owner. The list was alphabetized by city. The salesman had not bothered to wipe it. The first time I started the car I scrolled through the list out of curiosity. A hospital in Roseville. A florist in Davis. A wedding venue near Lake Tahoe. A small cemetery outside Reno. I had no plans to visit any of those places. Three weeks later my doctor sent me to the Roseville hospital for a scan. The address on the referral matched the saved destination exactly. I checked the GPS list when I got home. There it was.

sections.narration: The dealership was a small independent lot on Folsom Boulevard. About thirty cars on the lot at any time. The salesman was a man named Mike Petros. He had been selling cars there for nineteen years. He told me the Subaru had been a trade in from an estate executor. The executor had handed him the keys and asked for cash. Mike had given him eleven thousand dollars on the spot. The car had ninety four thousand miles, clean carfax, all maintenance records in the glove compartment. The previous owner had been a single owner who took good care of it. Mike did not know his name. He did not have any contact information for the executor.

sections.narration: I commute forty minutes each way to a job in Roseville. I drive the same loop every week. Grocery store on Tuesdays. Hardware store on Saturdays. I have lived in Sacramento for fifteen years. I have routines. I do not visit hospitals or cemeteries or wedding venues. The fourteen destinations in the GPS represented places I had never been and had no reason to go. I deleted six of them after the first week to free up space for my own home address and work address. I forgot about the rest. The Roseville hospital scan was the second time I had thought about the list since buying the car. I started paying closer attention to it after that.

sections.narration: Two weeks after the hospital trip my mother called. Her best friend's daughter was getting married in Davis and she wanted me to drive her to the bridal shower. The shower was being held at a florist on G Street. I drove my mother to the address. When I pulled up I recognized the storefront. I had seen it on the GPS list. I checked the list when I got back into the car. The florist's address was still saved as destination number five. I called Mike at the dealership. He confirmed the car had been on his lot for two months before I bought it. The destinations had been entered before he received the car.

sections.narration: I started keeping a journal. Every time I went to a new address I checked the GPS first. By August I had visited eleven of the fourteen destinations. The dentist on H Street where my regular dentist had referred me for a crown. The wedding venue near Lake Tahoe where my cousin held his ceremony. A small park in Folsom where a friend asked me to scatter his father's ashes. A funeral home in Elk Grove for a coworker's service. Each one had been on the list. None of them had been on my calendar when I bought the car. The list was not telling me where to go. The list already knew where I would go.

sections.narration: I requested the previous owner's information from the DMV using the VIN. The car had been registered to a man named Carl Bergquist of Stockton. He had owned it from new in two thousand eighteen until his death in September of last year. He had been sixty seven years old. The death certificate listed the cause as a heart attack. Carl had been a retired highway maintenance engineer. He had no children. He had been divorced for thirty years. He had a younger sister in Stockton named Inga. I drove to Stockton in September. Inga met me at a coffee shop on Pacific Avenue. She had been clearing out Carl's apartment for six months. She still had his belongings in storage.

sections.narration: Inga gave me a small leather diary from Carl's last year. The diary held a list of fourteen planned trips. Each entry was a destination and an intended arrival date. The destinations matched the addresses on the GPS exactly. The intended arrival dates matched the dates I had actually arrived at each location. I checked the journal I had been keeping. Hospital scan March twentieth. Diary entry hospital March twentieth. Florist on G Street April fourteenth. Diary entry florist on G Street April fourteenth. All eleven of my visits matched all eleven of Carl's intended visits to the day. Inga had no explanation. She told me her brother had been a careful planner but never a fortune teller.
image_prompts: close detail of a small leather diary open on a kitchen table showing handwritten entries with dates and addresses, dim ambient light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: Three of the saved destinations remain. I have not yet visited any of them. The first is a small clinic in Reno specializing in cardiology. The diary entry beside it is dated January seventeenth of next year. I have no cardiac issues that I am aware of. The second is a private residence in Truckee with no business listing or known occupant. The diary entry is dated April third. The third destination is a cemetery outside Reno called Mountain View. The diary entry beside it is dated August twentieth. The diary contains no further entries past August twentieth. The pages after that date are blank. Carl filled the diary in pen. The blank pages are not torn out.

sections.narration: I drove to Mountain View Cemetery in October. I parked outside the gates and walked in. Inga had told me Carl was buried at a different cemetery in Stockton. I walked the rows looking for whatever the destination might have been. About forty rows in I found a small headstone in the section reserved for prepaid plots. The plot had been purchased and the headstone installed but the burial had not yet taken place. The name engraved on the headstone was mine. My full first name. My middle name. My last name. The date of birth matched my own. The line for the date of death had been left blank. The maintenance log listed the headstone purchase date as November of two thousand twenty four.

sections.narration: I have not driven the Subaru in two months. It sits in my garage covered with a tarp. The GPS still holds three destinations. I have not deleted them. I have not been to a cardiologist. I have not visited Truckee. I have stopped looking at my calendar more than a week ahead. Inga has not returned my last two calls. The cemetery office sent me a letter offering to refund the prepaid plot if I would sign a release form. I have not signed it. The form lists the original purchaser as Carl Bergquist. Carl never met me. He died six months before I bought his car. The plot beside mine was prepaid by him also. The headstone reads his name.
